As a result, scenic plays were introduced, a novel concept for a warlike people who had previously only witnessed circus performances. Young men later adopted this behavior, exchanging humorous phrases and awkward rhymes with one another, accompanied by gestures that matched their conversation.

To the native performers, the name of histriones was given, because hister, in the Tuscan vocabulary, was the name of an actor who did not, as formerly, throw out alternately artless and unpolished verses like the Fescennine at random, but represented medleys complete with meter, the music being regularly adjusted for the musician, and with appropriate gesticulation. Livius, who several years later, gave up acting in medleys, was the first to attempt to tell a story with a regular plot. (The same technique was used by all at that time.) After breaking his voice from being called on too frequently and seeking permission, he is said to have placed a boy before the musician to sing, and performed the gesticulations with considerably freer movement, as his voice was not an impediment to him. The first introduction of plays was intended as a religious expiation. However, this did not relieve their minds from religious awe or their bodies from disease.
